title: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman previews the historic April 8, 2019 committee hearing for TX HB896, the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act, framing it as the first abortion abolition bill heard by a state legislative committee since Roe v. Wade and explaining what the bill does, who must vote for it, and how citizens can engage.
audiences: Pro-life activists weighing incrementalism vs. abolition

questions.question: Why is the April 8th hearing on TX HB896 historically significant?
questions.answer: It is positioned as the first time since Roe v. Wade that a true abortion abolition bill will receive a hearing before a state legislative committee, breaking a 46-year pattern in which legislatures only considered regulatory or incremental pro-life bills.
questions.quote: Texas will make history on Monday as the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act (TX HB896) is scheduled to become the first abortion abolition bill to be heard by a legislative committee since the Supreme Court opined that all 50 states should allow abortion in 1973.

questions.question: How does HB896 differ from the typical pro-life bill being passed in 2019?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: Unlike the more than 250 pro-life bills introduced in 2019 that produce minimal change, HB896 would actually end abortion in Texas by extending equal legal protection to preborn humans rather than merely regulating the killing.
questions.quote: There have been more than 250 pro-life bills introduced in State legislatures across the country in 2019 alone, most of which accomplish little-to-nothing. This is not one of those bills. TX HB896 would be the end of abortion in Texas.

questions.question: What constitutional principle does HB896 invoke to grant preborn children legal personhood?
questions.answer: The bill borrows language from the 14th Amendment's equal protection guarantee, declaring that a living human child from the moment of fertilization is entitled to the same rights and protections as any other child under Texas law.
questions.quote: A living human child, from the moment of fertilization on fusion of a human spermatozoon with a human ovum, is entitled to the same rights, powers, and privileges as are secured or granted by the laws of this state to any other human child.

questions.question: How does the bill handle conflicting federal authority such as Roe v. Wade?
questions.answer: HB896 directs the Texas Attorney General and state subdivisions to enforce homicide and assault statutes against abortion regardless of contrary federal action, and it declares any federal law, executive order, or court ruling to the contrary void under both the Texas and US Constitutions.
questions.quote: Any federal law, executive order, or court decision that purports to supersede, stay, or overrule this Act is in violation of the Texas Constitution and the United States Constitution and is therefore void.

questions.question: Which committee members will determine whether HB896 advances?
questions.answer: The bill needs all five Republicans on the House Judiciary and Civil Jurisprudence Committee. James White is a co-sponsor and chairman Jeff Leach previously sponsored identical legislation, so the swing votes are Matt Krause, Morgan Meyer, and Reggie Smith, whose positions are publicly unknown.
questions.quote: The leanings of Reps. Matt Krause (R-Fort Worth), Morgan Meyer (R-Dallas), and Reggie Smith (R-Sherman) are unknown. Without their unanimous support, TX HB896 will die, and with it thousands of preborn human beings.

questions.question: What concrete action does Silberman ask Texans and out-of-state supporters to take?
questions.answer: Texans able to attend should show up at the Capitol with a two-to-three minute prepared speech to deliver testimony for the bill, while non-Texans should follow Abolish Abortion Texas's Facebook live stream of the hearing.
questions.quote: There will be a time to give testimony on behalf of the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act. Come prepared with a two-to-three minute speech and you will be able to speak on behalf of the vulnerable preborn directly to those whose responsibility it is to protect them.

questions.question: Why does the title cite 110,000 lives rather than the annual abortion total?
questions.answer: Because the Texas legislature meets only every other year, the failure of HB896 in 2019 would mean roughly two years' worth of Texas abortions, around 110,000 preborn babies, before the next opportunity to outlaw the practice.
questions.quote: About 55,000 babies are murdered annually in Texas. With the State legislature meeting every other year, the fate of 110,000 babies rests with TX HB896.

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: Direct follow-up reporting the outcome after committee chairman Jeff Leach killed HB896, fulfilling the warning issued in this preview.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Later 2019 piece exposing how Texas's pro-life political establishment, including Leach and Abbott, opposed the abolition effort previewed here.

related.title: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
related.reason: Shows how Texas abolitionists regrouped after HB896's defeat to embed abolition in the state GOP platform, continuing the legislative arc begun here.

related.title: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
related.reason: Explores prominent pro-life opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act, illustrating the intra-movement battle this article foreshadows.

related.title: Alaska State Reps. Introduce Abortion Abolition Bill
related.reason: Parallel state-level abolition bill introduced shortly after, showing the legislative momentum the Texas hearing helped catalyze.

related.title: WATCH: Wesley Thomas' Brilliant Testimony at the TX GOP State Convention
related.reason: Concrete example of the kind of public testimony for abolition that this article calls Texans to deliver at the April 8th hearing.

related.title: Don't Fall For Texas Right to Life's Faux Abolitionism
related.reason: Contrasts true equal-protection bills like HB896 with watered-down pro-life legislation, sharpening the distinction Silberman draws against the '250 pro-life bills' baseline.
